lesson.title: Standard Library Tour
lesson.summary: A guided tour of essential Go standard library packages.

sections.heading: Strings, numbers, and time
sections.bodyHtml:
The strings package provides string manipulation:

strings.Contains("hello", "ll")    // true
strings.ToUpper("go")              // "GO"
strings.TrimSpace("  hi  ")        // "hi"
strings.Join([]string{"a", "b"}, ",") // "a,b"

strconv converts strings to numbers and back:

n, _ := strconv.Atoi("42")          // 42
s := strconv.Itoa(42)               // "42"

time handles dates, durations, and formatting:

now := time.Now()
fmt.Println(now.Format("2006-01-02")) // current date

sections.heading: Sorting, flags, and logging
sections.bodyHtml:
sort sorts built-in and custom types:

nums := []int{3, 1, 4, 1, 5}
sort.Ints(nums)  // [1 1 3 4 5]

flag parses command-line arguments:

var name = flag.String("name", "world", "a name")
flag.Parse()
fmt.Println("Hello,", *name)

log provides timestamped output:

log.Println("server starting")

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml:
Open practice/stdlib/main.go.

Write a program that reads a comma-separated string, splits it with strings.Split, sorts the parts with sort.Strings, and joins them back with strings.Join. Print the result.

questions.prompt: Which package converts a string like "42" to an int?
What reference date is used in Go's time formatting?
How do you sort a slice of ints in ascending order?

exercises.title: Sort comma-separated words
exercises.instructions: Split a comma-separated string, sort the words, join them back, and print.
